Main Characteristics: Allowing users to feel benefits before asking for investment or personal data.
Do's: Let users engage with core features or browse products before asking them to sign up or log in.
Don'ts: Don't present registration/sign-in as a roadblock at the start of the experience.
Severity: Critical - High impact on user acquisition and initial conversion.

Main Characteristics: Use of the design is easy to understand, regardless of experience, knowledge, or language skills.
Do's: Eliminate unnecessary complexity; be consistent with user expectations and intuition.
Don'ts: Not in source
Severity: Critical - Directly affects user abandonment and conversion rates.

Main Characteristics: The design communicates necessary information effectively to the user regardless of conditions.
Do's: Use different modes (pictorial, verbal, tactile) for redundant presentation of essential information.
Don'ts: Not in source
Severity: Critical - Essential for navigating the purchase funnel.

Main Characteristics: Highest priority is satisfying the customer through early delivery of valuable software.
Do's: Release updates frequently to meet changing customer needs.
Don'ts: Do not prioritize internal feature lists over actual customer satisfaction.
Severity: Critical - Direct impact on customer satisfaction and revenue.

Main Characteristics: Continuous attention to good design enhances agility.
Do's: Continuously refine the design throughout the cycle based on feedback.
Don'ts: Do not settle on a design specification and refuse to revisit it during implementation.
Severity: Critical - Good design is essential for market success and usability.

Main Characteristics: Team reflects on how to become more effective and adjusts behavior.
Do's: Identify areas where design-dev communication failed and propose specific fixes.
Don'ts: Do not skip retrospectives or ignore team feedback regarding the design process.
Severity: Critical - Necessary for the survival and improvement of the Agile process.

Main Characteristics: The visual design of a button should clearly indicate its interactivity to avoid confusion with non-actionable elements.
Do's: Make the button stand out; use familiar shapes like rectangles or rounded rectangles.
Don'ts: Do not remove so many elements that the function dissolves; do not use organic or irregular shapes that take longer to identify.

Main Characteristics: Interactive elements must be large enough to be easily activated by fingers or cursors.
Do's: Start with a minimum of  $44 \times 44$  points for mobile; target 50 points for mobile buttons; use  $32 \times 32$  for cursor-based devices.
Don'ts: Do not design buttons that are too small, which leads to user frustration and app uninstalls.

Main Characteristics: The degree of reorientation a person experiences when moving between pages/touchpoints.
Do's: Ensure consistency across touchpoints so users don't have to bridge gaps in the offering themselves.
Don'ts: Don't force people to reorient constantly; high volatility leads to users feeling lost in 'hyperspace'.

Main Characteristics: Comforting screens that occur when things go wrong without destroying user data.
Do's: Keep all user input safe; provide a clear path to resolution and use human, positive language.
Don'ts: Do not use 'Blue Screen of Death' style jargon; never delete user work due to a form error.

Main Characteristics: Placing primary controls within the natural sweeping arc of a user's thumb.
Do's: Place primary actions and navigation near the bottom of the screen for large mobile devices.
Don'ts: Avoid placing critical navigation (like 'Back' or 'Purchase') in the top-left 'Ow' zone.

Principle: Proper Product Data is Vital to E-Commerce Usability
Main Characteristics: Consistency in structured data, accurate product tags, and complete product information.
Do's: Develop automated and manual verification processes; harmonize product data from different suppliers; scan for missing thumbnails; printed site-hierarchy trees.
Don'ts: Store product specs as unstructured blobs of text; rely solely on machine-generated categories; use vendor-supplied naming inconsistencies without post-processing.

Principle: Which Attributes to Include in the List Item
Main Characteristics: Providing enough info to decide whether to skip or explore a product.
Do's: Include price, thumbnail, title/type, variations, and user ratings; add 1-3 category-specific attributes (e.g., dimensions for bags).
Don'ts: Overload the list item with too much information; use a 'one size fits all' attribute set for all categories.

Principle: Product Variations as One or Multiple List Items
Main Characteristics: Strategy for displaying color, size, or material versions of a single product.
Do's: Combine variations into a single list item for minor or non-visual differences; display separate list items for meaningful visual differences (patterns, textures).
Don'ts: Clutter the list by showing every size or format as a separate item; fail to link variations in the database.

Principle: Include Product Attributes Consistently Across List Items
Main Characteristics: Ensuring comparability between items in the same list.
Do's: Explicitly state when an attribute is unavailable rather than hiding it; harmonize measurements (e.g., all in cm).
Don'ts: Show specs only for some products; use inconsistent labeling (e.g., 'Height' vs. 'Depth') for similar products.

Principle: Product Specifications Should Be Separate Entities
Main Characteristics: Structuring information for scannability.
Do's: Distinguish product type from name using font weights or colors; use bullet points for key features.
Don'ts: Mix brand, type, and specs into a single unstructured text blob in the title.

Main Characteristics: Preventing detours to products that cannot be shipped or bought.
Do's: Use high-contrast badges (e.g., 'Sold Out'); state when the item will be back in stock; mention pre-order options.
Don'ts: Use small, easily overlooked text labels; hide unavailability until the user reaches the product page.

Principle: Always Have Thumbnails for All List Items
Main Characteristics: Visual anchors for product assessment.
Do's: Ensure every single list item has an image; prioritize image quality.
Don'ts: Show a 'No image available' placeholder; expect users to click items without a preview.

Main Characteristics: Removing intermediary steps that confuse site hierarchy.
Do's: Invest in better list item design and larger thumbnails instead; make the Quick View a gallery if it must exist.
Don'ts: Place the Quick View button in the middle of the thumbnail where it causes accidental clicks; make the Quick View look exactly like a product page.

Principle: Avoid Implementing Filter Attributes as Category Scopes
Main Characteristics: Distinction between hierarchy (mutually exclusive) and filters (combinable).
Do's: Implement attributes like 'material' or 'season' as filters; use categories only for distinct product types with unique spec sets.
Don'ts: Force users into silos like 'Mummy Shape Sleeping Bags' vs 'Square Shape' categories.

Principle: Allow Users to Combine Multiple Filter Values of the Same Filter Type
Main Characteristics: Multi-select capabilities within groups like color or brand.
Do's: Use 'OR' logic for values within a type; use checkboxes to signal that multiple items can be selected.
Don'ts: Reload the page and clear selections when a second value in the same group is clicked.

Principle: Diversity-Based 'Relevance' Should Be the Default Sort Type
Main Characteristics: Ensuring the first page represents the full breadth of a category.
Do's: Show 1-2 products of each major sub-type in the first 20 results; use a matrix of sales, ratings, and views.
Don'ts: Sort by price by default; allow a single popular sub-brand to dominate the entire first page of results.

Main Characteristics: First-time users rely on homepage content and main navigation to determine site type and product breadth.
Do's: Manifest the breadth of the product catalog by ensuring a broad selection of different product types are featured with images or thumbnails.
Don'ts: Don't feature only a small or overly narrow selection of product types on the homepage, as users may assume the site doesn't carry other items.

Main Characteristics: Categories are mutually exclusive hierarchy parts; filters narrow lists by combining attributes.
Do's: Implement product type, brand, and style as filters when attributes are shared. Use categories to create defined scopes for specific filtering.
Don'ts: Don't implement product attributes as mutually exclusive categories, as this prevents users from mixing criteria (e.g., 'down' and 'mummy').

Main Characteristics: Ambiguity between options confuses users and damages brand trust.
Do's: Manually plan and agree upon the site taxonomy. Group symptoms or solutions separately rather than combining them into vague categories.
Don'ts: Don't use machine-generated or raw manufacturer categorization without merging and renaming. Avoid identical names like having two 'Sports' categories.

Main Characteristics: Users struggle to verify compatibility for technical accessories (e.g., camera cases, laptop chargers).
Do's: Create unique permanent pages for all compatible accessories for specific models/series. Cross-link these from product and cart pages.
Don'ts: Don't suggest products as 'recommended' unless compatibility is verified or the source category is linked.

Principle: Avoid relying solely on thematic categories
Main Characteristics: Purely thematic navigation breaks the mental model of browsing by product type.
Do's: Ensure the main navigation supports finding items by type (e.g., 'Jackets', 'Shoes').
Don'ts: Don't use only featured, thematic, or brand-based links in the main navigation.

Principle: The main navigation categories should be the first level of product categories
Main Characteristics: Hiding categories behind a single 'Products' link adds a needless layer and hinders site inference.
Do's: Place top-level product categories in the permanently visible main navigation bar.
Don'ts: Don't use a single navigation item like 'Products' or 'Catalog' to house the entire taxonomy, as it causes double-hover issues.

Main Characteristics: Users often mistake category thumbnails for specific products.
Do's: Crop thumbnails so they don't depict products in their entirety or use multi-product images to show category breadth.
Don'ts: Don't depict a single specific product clearly, as users may think the link leads to that item only. Avoid thumbnails of products in packaging.

Principle: Avoid dividing or sub-dividing product lists
Main Characteristics: Users baffle at divided lists and often fail to realize they are viewing only a fraction of the selection.
Do's: Display featured products in 1-2 rows without pagination or sliders and link to the source category.
Don'ts: Don't use embedded product sliders or tabs on sub-category pages. Don't divide product lists into thematic sections.

Principle: The list layout should match the product type
Main Characteristics: Spec-driven vs. Visually-driven products require different information density.
Do's: Use grid-view for visually driven industries (apparel, furniture) to allow for large thumbnails. Use list-view for spec-driven products (electronics, appliances).
Don'ts: Don't use small thumbnails for visually driven products, as it increases pogo-sticking.

Principle: Default sort type should indicate category breadth
Main Characteristics: Initial results shape the user's perception of category boundaries.
Do's: Use a default 'Relevance' sort that combines sales data, pageviews, and product type diversity.
Don'ts: Don't sort by 'Best Selling' by default if it clusters only one product type (e.g., all Star Wars toys) at the top.

Main Characteristics: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
Do's: Provide the same means of use for all users: identical whenever possible; equivalent when not.
Don'ts: Avoid segregating or stigmatizing any users.
Severity: High - Essential for conversion across diverse user segments.

Main Characteristics: Replicating the same basic experience across different devices.
Do's: Keep content, flow, structure, and core feature set consistent across the ecosystem.
Don'ts: Avoid disjointed UIs that make the service feel like a collection of separate tools.
Severity: High - Maintains user trust and facilitates seamless cross-device shopping.

Main Characteristics: Eliminating work required of the user beyond giving consent.
Do's: Use intelligent defaults and automate repeated actions after initial informed consent.
Don'ts: Don't use behind-the-scenes trickery; always ensure user consent is obtained first.
Severity: High - Dramatically increases task completion by removing friction.

Main Characteristics: Harnessing change for the customer's competitive advantage, even late in development.
Do's: Solicit and act on late-cycle customer feedback; treat changes as helpful information.
Don'ts: Do not resist changes or defer them to unspecified future releases just to stick to a plan.
Severity: High - Ensures the product remains relevant in a fast-moving market.

Main Characteristics: Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple of months.
Do's: Provide working code for usability testing early in the cycle.
Don'ts: Do not rely exclusively on rough prototypes until the end of the release cycle.
Severity: High - Increases opportunities for research and design validation.

Main Characteristics: The most efficient method of conveying information is in-person discussion.
Do's: Use whiteboards for collaborative design; use video chat if teams are remote.
Don'ts: Do not rely solely on email or instant messaging for complex design concepts.
Severity: High - Essential for shared understanding of complex design problems.

Main Characteristics: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
Do's: Measure UX progress by the implementation of research findings into the live product.
Don'ts: Do not judge UX success solely by the number of wireframes or artifacts produced.
Severity: High - Focuses effort on what the customer actually experiences.

Principle: What you see isn't what your brain gets
Main Characteristics: The brain constantly interprets visual input and creates shortcuts based on past experience and expectations.
Do's: Persuade people to see things in a certain way based on presentation.
Don'ts: Assume people will see what you think they will see on a web page.

Principle: Peripheral vision is used more than central vision to get the gist of a scene
Main Characteristics: Peripheral vision provides the overall context (gist) of what a user is looking at.
Do's: Make sure the information in the periphery communicates the purpose of the page and site.
Don'ts: Put animation or blinking elements in the peripheral vision if you want users to concentrate on the center.

Principle: There's a special part of the brain just for recognizing faces
Main Characteristics: Faces are processed more quickly than other objects via the fusiform face area (FFA).
Do's: Use faces looking at the user for emotional impact; use faces looking at a product to direct attention.
Don'ts: Assume people with autism will process faces through the same channels as non-autistic users.

Principle: People scan screens based on past experience and expectations
Main Characteristics: Users look at the center of the screen and avoid edges; they follow cultural reading patterns.
Do's: Put the most important information in the top third or middle of the screen.
Don'ts: Put important information at the edges of the screen.

Principle: People see cues that tell them what to do (Affordances)
Main Characteristics: Physical or visual cues (shadows, shading) suggest how to interact with an object.
Do's: Use shading to show when an object is chosen or active; use button-like shadows.
Don'ts: Provide incorrect affordance cues (e.g., a handle that says 'Push' but looks like a 'Pull').

Principle: People can miss changes in their visual fields
Main Characteristics: Inattention blindness occurs when users fail to notice large changes if they are focused on a different task.
Do's: Use visual cues like blinking or sounds to ensure users notice a critical change.
Don'ts: Assume people will see something on a screen just because it appeared there.

Main Characteristics: 9% of men and 0.5% of women have color deficiency, most commonly red-green.
Do's: Use redundant coding (color + line thickness/shapes); check sites with color-blindness filters.
Don'ts: Rely solely on color to give specific meaning.

Main Characteristics: Readability is affected by existing knowledge, titles, and text structure.
Do's: Provide meaningful titles and headlines; use simple words and shorter syllables.
Don'ts: Assume people will remember specific information just because they read it.

Main Characteristics: Text must be large enough to read without strain; x-height affects perceived size.
Do's: Use a large point size and a font with a large x-height for online viewing.
Don'ts: Use small fonts that require users to squint, as this creates a negative mood.

Main Characteristics: Working memory is fragile and easily disrupted by sensory input.
Do's: Keep information in one place so users don't have to remember it across pages.
Don'ts: Ask users to do another task while holding information in working memory.

Principle: People remember only four items at once
Main Characteristics: The true limit of working memory is four items, not seven.
Do's: Chunk information into groups of no more than four items.
Don'ts: List more than four items in a single group without further categorization.

Main Characteristics: It is easier for the brain to recognize information using context than to pull it from memory.
Do's: Use icons and menus to eliminate memory load; provide context.
Don'ts: Require users to remember and type in information from a previous screen.

Main Characteristics: Providing only the information needed at the moment prevents cognitive overload.
Do's: Show people what they need when they need it; use clicks to reveal more detail.
Don'ts: Count clicks as the primary metric of success; the clarity of the path is more important.

Principle: People are more motivated as they get closer to a goal
Main Characteristics: The goal-gradient effect states that behavior accelerates as the goal comes into sight.
Do's: Show progress bars; provide an 'illusion of progress' (e.g., a 10-stamp card with 2 pre-filled).
Don'ts: Ignore the post-reward resetting phenomenon where motivation drops after a goal is met.

Main Characteristics: Unpredictable rewards (variable ratio) result in the highest rate of behavior repetition.
Do's: Use unpredictable rewards for maximum engagement (e.g., slot machine mechanics).
Don'ts: Use fixed rewards if you want to maintain long-term information-seeking behavior.

Main Characteristics: Humans 'satisfice,' meaning they look for the good-enough solution rather than the optimal one.
Do's: Design for scanning; use white space and large fonts to make a task look 'easy'.
Don'ts: Provide too many options that require heavy analysis.

Main Characteristics: The most important actionable element tied to sales must be visually unique.
Do's: Use a unique color or style; ensure high contrast against the background; use actionable wording.
Don'ts: Do not use more than one CTA on a single page as it divides user attention and lowers conversion; do not make it too small or so big it causes 'banner blindness'.

Main Characteristics: Placement of buttons should follow natural scanning patterns to facilitate the flow to checkout.
Do's: Place buttons below descriptive elements; align them to the sides where users expect them; follow the F-pattern (top to bottom, left edge).
Don'ts: Do not place the strongest visual element in a position that requires the eyes to travel back and forth excessively, which slows processing.

Main Characteristics: Navigation patterns should accommodate how users physically hold and reach across mobile devices.
Do's: Use bottom tab menus for high accessibility; place primary controls in areas easily accessible by the thumb.
Don'ts: Do not place primary navigation in the top-left or top-right corners (like hamburger menus) as they are the least accessible for one-handed use.

Main Characteristics: Each piece is part of a greater whole and should contribute positively to the system at scale. No isolated features or outliers.
Do's: Ensure every component addresses all four design principles to maintain a cohesive ecosystem.
Don'ts: Do not create isolated features or design elements that do not fit into the broader system architecture.

Main Characteristics: Agnostic to device and accessible to all; designed for a global family across 27 languages.
Do's: Consider the entire extended family of users and ensure cross-platform compatibility.
Don'ts: Do not design for a single device type or ignore accessibility and language requirements.

Main Characteristics: Prioritizes longevity and proven utility over current stylistic trends.
Do's: Adopt technology or design elements only if they serve a proven purpose for as many users as possible.
Don'ts: Do not introduce stylistic features like parallax effects just because they are trendy.

Main Characteristics: Clarity of focus on a single primary action or piece of information.
Do's: Explicitly decide what you want the user to see and do first on a given screen.
Don'ts: Do not present multiple competing priorities that confuse the user's focus.

Main Characteristics: Designed for exploration where errors or mis-taps are impossible or non-destructive.
Do's: Design interfaces to be as resilient as a children's toy, encouraging user exploration.
Don'ts: Do not create fragile layouts where a single mis-click causes frustration or lost progress.

Main Characteristics: Design is based on research, data, and actual user requirements rather than assumptions.
Do's: Analyze data and talk to users to identify the real problem before building a solution.
Don'ts: Do not make assumptions about what the user wants without empirical evidence.

Main Characteristics: The hierarchy of priorities where clarity and efficiency always precede aesthetic concerns.
Do's: Ensure the message of a talk or item is fully legible even if it requires a less 'perfect' layout.
Don'ts: Do not clip or hide important content (like long titles) just to maintain a clean visual look.

Main Characteristics: Clear and to the point; easy-to-follow actions that do not distract from the primary task.
Do's: Use motion to simplify the user experience and respect the user's time.
Don'ts: Do not use motion that distracts from the primary task.

Main Characteristics: Animation used with intention to surface connections between states or provide feedback.
Do's: Use animation to draw attention to important details or help users reach goals.
Don'ts: Do not use animation without a functional purpose.

Main Characteristics: Fits naturally into the experience without creating distraction.
Do's: Use just enough motion to get the intention across and no more.
Don'ts: Avoid excessive motion that creates visual noise.

Main Characteristics: Creates a sense of efficiency and responsiveness while remaining subtle.
Do's: Use for micro-interactions like button states, dropdowns, and data table rendering.
Don'ts: Do not use dramatic or slow animations in task-heavy productivity contexts.

Main Characteristics: Entrances should inform exits to establish spatial orientation.
Do's: Animate objects back to their origin point (e.g., if it entered from bottom, exit to bottom).
Don'ts: Do not have objects disappear in a direction unrelated to their entry, which confuses the user's mental map.

Main Characteristics: The 'zenith' of the product's potential with maximum value and content.
Do's: Start design with the ideal state to set the tone for all other states.
Don'ts: Do not design empty or error states without first defining the ideal functional state.

Main Characteristics: The state seen when there is no data to show (first use, cleared data, or no results).
Do's: Tell the user exactly which button to press and why; use stand-in content to show potential.
Don'ts: Avoid saying 'Nothing to see here'; do not use oppressive 'coach marks' that cover the UI.

Main Characteristics: Using contrast and size to direct attention to important elements.
Do's: Make more important things bigger and higher contrast.
Don'ts: Don't 'make the logo bigger' unless it is the most important element for the user's current goal.

Main Characteristics: Designing for 'smart but busy' users and being considerate of human frailties.
Do's: Focus on 'intermediates' who want to get a task done quickly without feeling like a novice.
Don'ts: Do not force users to memorize abstract icons; use clear labels where possible.

Main Characteristics: Distinguishing between primary, secondary, and tertiary actions to guide users effectively.
Do's: Use solid, high-contrast background colors for primary actions to make them obvious.
Don'ts: Don't design actions based purely on semantics (e.g., making every 'delete' button big and red if it's not the primary action).

Main Characteristics: Reducing clutter by removing redundant labels when formats or context are clear.
Do's: Use formatting (like $ or @) to imply the nature of data; combine labels into values (e.g., '12 left in stock').
Don'ts: Don't use a 'label: value' format as a default; it gives equal emphasis to all data and creates noise.

Main Characteristics: Ensuring text legibility over busy background images in hero sections.
Do's: Add a semi-transparent overlay (dark for light text) or use a text shadow with a large blur radius.
Don'ts: Don't slap a headline over a raw hero image without checking contrast across both light and dark areas.

Main Characteristics: Turning empty views into opportunities for engagement and conversion.
Do's: Incorporate illustrations and clear call-to-action buttons (e.g., 'Add First Item') in empty states.
Don'ts: Don't settle for 'No items found' text; it is boring and provides no path forward for the user.

Main Characteristics: Guide design decisions by finding out user needs and goals.
Do's: Structure products so tasks can be carried out as quickly as possible; bear in mind what is suitable for the specific demographic.
Don'ts: Do not ignore the technical savvy or age of the target audience (e.g., avoid flat interfaces for non-tech-savvy older users).

Main Characteristics: Inclusive design for all users, including those with sight, hearing, touch, or cognitive impairments.
Do's: Build accessibility in from the start; use larger legible text; associate form fields with labels; add alt text to images.
Don'ts: Do not rely on one sense alone (e.g., sound-only alerts); do not assume accessibility only benefits people with disabilities.

Main Characteristics: Ensuring the product doesn't break under real-world data stress.
Do's: Check designs with long blocks of text, translations, and missing or varied images.
Don'ts: Do not only design for 'best-case' or pretty artwork that will break in production.

Main Characteristics: Using layout, color, and typography to influence the order in which information is consumed.
Do's: Grab attention with high contrast and large, bold type; push less important items back with low contrast.
Don'ts: Do not ignore reading patterns (e.g., top-left for Western cultures).

Main Characteristics: Ensuring text is comfortable to read on digital screens.
Do's: Use at least 12pt (16pt/1em recommended); set line spacing (leading) to 1.5x font size; keep line lengths between 45-75 characters.
Don'ts: Avoid serif fonts for visually impaired users; don't use all-caps for long paragraphs; don't use moving or blinking text.

Main Characteristics: Minimizing mistakes through design and providing ways to fix them.
Do's: Use warnings and confirmations for hazardous actions; provide 'undo' or 'restore' options; place labels above input fields.
Don'ts: Do not allow users to cruise through hazardous procedures on 'autopilot'; don't hide labels inside input fields once data is entered.

Main Characteristics: Ensuring interface elements are easily operable by human fingers.
Do's: Use a minimum touch area of  $7\text{mm} \times 7\text{mm}$ ; leave at least a 2mm gap between items; make thumb-operated items 25mm wide.
Don'ts: Do not place interactive elements too close together, leading to accidental presses.

Main Characteristics: Maintaining predictable movement and placement across the application.
Do's: Position navigational elements in the same place on all screens; clearly title pages; minimize steps (aim for within 4 pages).
Don'ts: Do not switch button actions in different parts of the app; avoid making pages so long users 'endlessly flick up'.

Main Characteristics: Confirming user actions through visual or auditory cues.
Do's: Use spinners or progress messages for loading states; use pressed states for buttons; provide feedback in multiple forms (visual + sound).
Don'ts: Do not leave a user wondering if an app has frozen during processing.

Main Characteristics: The design accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
Do's: Provide choice in methods of use; accommodate right- or left-handed access and usage.
Don'ts: Not in source
Severity: High - Impacts task completion efficiency for diverse users.

Main Characteristics: The design minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental actions.
Do's: Arrange elements to minimize hazards and errors: most used elements, most accessible; hazardous elements eliminated, isolated, or shielded.
Don'ts: Not in source
Severity: High - Prevents accidental checkout abandonment or incorrect orders.

Principle: When to Use a List View, a Grid View, or Both
Main Characteristics: Optimizing product presentation based on whether a purchase is spec-driven or visually-driven.
Do's: Use List View for spec-driven products (electronics, appliances); use Grid View for visually-driven products (apparel, furniture); switch automatically based on context.
Don'ts: Use a one-size-fits-all approach for mixed product catalogs; hide prices in mobile grid views.

Principle: Show Total Number of Items in the Currently Displayed Product List
Main Characteristics: Visibility of total result count to gauge search query or category scope quality.
Do's: Integrate the number into the page header or product list title; show count both above and below the list; use explanatory labels (e.g., 'x products match').
Don'ts: Position count where it can be mistaken for pagination links; hide the total count from plain sight.

Principle: Have Hierarchy-Based Breadcrumbs in Category Product Lists
Main Characteristics: Sense of space and site position for users traversing hierarchies.
Do's: Include hierarchy-based breadcrumbs on category pages; consider 'Sideways Breadcrumbs' for sibling category navigation.
Don'ts: Provide breadcrumbs only on product pages.

Main Characteristics: Impact of non-standard scrolling or navigation on focus.
Do's: Use novel navigation only for inspirational storytelling content; stick to standard scrolling for product lists.
Don'ts: Use parallax scrolling or non-standard methods for actual product selection lists.
